pattern: Since one popcount application already shrinks any huge number down to at most a few hundred, precomputing how many steps every small number needs to reach 1 turns the real question into 'count numbers below n whose bit-count lands in an allowed set', which a digit DP over the binary string answers directly.
hints: Popcount(x) is at most the number of bits of x, so after just one operation any x, however large, becomes a small number (at most a few hundred). Precompute, for every small value m, how many extra popcount steps it needs to reach 1.

x itself is k-reducible exactly when popcount(x) belongs to the set of small values whose precomputed step count is at most k - 1 (one step is already spent turning x into popcount(x), unless x is already 1).

Counting integers below n with a popcount in a fixed target set is a classic digit-DP over n's binary digits: at every '1' bit of n, consider placing a '0' there instead and freely choosing the remaining lower bits, using binomial coefficients to count how many ways reach each target popcount.

edge_inputs.description: k of exactly 1 excludes numbers that need two popcount steps
a long run of set bits pushes many numbers just out of reach for a small k

stress.description: random binary strings (no leading zero) with a random k
